text.title: Survey Design Process
text.summary: Six-step process for creating surveys that yield valid, actionable quantitative data.

rule.statement: Conduct at least 10 qualitative interviews before creating a survey.
rule.rationale: Surveys quantify known patterns; they cannot discover unknown problems. Without prior interviews, you cannot write valid questions — you do not yet know what to ask.

section.title: Step 1 — Define Research Objective

rule.statement: Define one specific research question, the decision it informs, the target audience, and a minimum sample size before writing a single survey question.
rule.rationale: Surveys without a defined objective drift toward vague data that cannot drive any decision. A crisp objective statement also prevents scope creep during question writing.

section.reference: Survey design document template with objective section

section.title: Step 2 — Choose Question Types
section.p: Select the question type that best fits each research goal:

list.item: Multiple choice: discrete, mutually exclusive options ("How often do you...?")
Rating scale (5-point general, 7-point nuanced, 10-point NPS, binary yes/no)
Open-ended: one or two per survey maximum, placed at the end
Matrix: multiple items against the same scale (satisfaction across features)
NPS (0-10): loyalty and recommendation intent only

section.title: Step 3 — Write Questions

rule.statement: Each question must be specific, single-concept, neutrally worded, and answerable from direct experience — not hypothetical intent.
rule.rationale: Double-barreled items ("How easy and useful is X?") split respondent attention and make data uninterpretable. Hypothetical questions ("Would you pay X?") over-predict actual behavior by 2-5x.

example.code: Bad: "How often will you use this feature?" Why: hypothetical future intent, not past behavior Bad: "How satisfied and engaged are you?" Why: double-barreled — two separate questions fused Bad: "Don't you think X is better than Y?" Why: leading — presupposes a preferred answer
Good: "How often have you used similar tools in the last 30 days?" Good: "How satisfied are you with [product]?" + separate "How engaged do you feel?" Good: "Which do you prefer, X or Y?"

section.reference: Per-research-type question banks (pricing, satisfaction, feature prioritization)

rule.statement: Keep total completion time under 7 minutes; longer surveys drop below 50% completion. Place screener first, demographics last, open-ended questions at the end.
rule.rationale: Completion rates drop sharply past 7 minutes. Screener questions at the front disqualify off-target respondents before they waste time on core questions. Demographics last reduces abandonment from privacy-sensitive openers.

list.item: Screener (1-2 questions): qualify respondents, route off-target out
Attention check at Q5: "Select 'Somewhat agree' to confirm you are reading"

section.title: Step 5 — Test and Distribute

rule.statement: Pilot with 5-10 people from the actual audience and measure median completion time. If median exceeds 7 minutes, cut questions until it does not.
rule.rationale: Self-estimated completion time is always optimistic. Piloting reveals confusing items, technical issues, and timing reality before the main field period.

section.p: Distribution channel trade-offs: email list (qualified but biased to existing users), social media (broad reach but self-selection), paid panels (fast and targeted but expensive and variable quality), in-product (high completion but only active users), communities (engaged and specific but may be small).

rule.statement: For paid panels, do not let an agent auto-launch fielding — cost spikes and ethics review require a human approval gate.
rule.rationale: Automated panel launches can incur significant unexpected costs and may violate panel provider terms if attention-check criteria are not pre-configured.

rule.statement: Deduplicate on (IP, device fingerprint, completion time < 30% of median) before any analysis. Paid panels return 5-30% bot or satisficer responses.
rule.rationale: Drawing conclusions from unfiltered panel data overstates real market preferences and invalidates statistical significance claims.

list.item: Quantitative: response distributions, cross-tabulations by segment, statistical significance
Qualitative (open-ended): theme coding with counts, verbatim quotes per theme required
Report open-ended analysis with the actual corpus size explicitly stated
